behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401" and "body is not forwarded." Slot B: handoff boundary (HTTP handler -> signature verifier -> queue publisher). Slot C: if lowered to Unit, a verifier-only test can pass while wiring still forwards to queue on auth failure. REJECTION-GATE: considered System, rejected because behavior text only requires response code + no forward, not real network/OS effects.
behaviors.test_description: Run the handler with a realistic request object containing raw body bytes and a bad `X-Signature-256`, with a spy/fake queue publisher. Assert `401` and zero enqueue calls in the same request path.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned." Slot B: handoff/ordering boundary between queue write completion and HTTP response send. Slot C: if lowered to Unit, local function assertions can miss mis-ordered awaits across modules where `res.send` fires before durable write promise resolves. REJECTION-GATE: considered System, rejected because the behavior text is ordering semantics, not real OS/network/subprocess behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Wire real handler + queue adapter (or transactional test double exposing write completion), inject controlled write latency, and capture timestamps/order of `queue.write` completion vs `res.status(200).send`. Assert queue write resolves first.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing ... to finish." Slot B: async handoff boundary (enqueue/dispatch module vs downstream worker module). Slot C: if lowered to Unit, you can miss an accidental `await` on downstream processing introduced in handler orchestration. REJECTION-GATE: considered System, rejected because this can be proven with in-process deferred downstream promises; no real network/OS primitive is required.
behaviors.test_description: Use a downstream processor stub that blocks on a controllable promise. Send request, assert response is returned before unblocking processor, then unblock and assert processor eventually completes.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "within 3 seconds wall-clock time" and "slow and concurrent." Slot B: real-infra boundary (actual HTTP server sockets, event loop scheduling, OS timing under concurrency). Slot C: if lowered to Integration, in-process/fake-timer tests can miss real event-loop starvation and socket backlog delays that breach 3s in production. REJECTION-GATE: considered Agentic, rejected because behavior text has no AI/ML output dependence.
behaviors.test_description: Start the service process, generate concurrent prior events with intentionally slow downstream work, then send fresh webhook requests over real HTTP and measure end-to-end wall-clock latency. Assert p95 (or strict per-request bound per spec) stays <= 3s.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "ID was already received is not written ... a second time." Slot B: handoff/state boundary between dedup logic and persistent queue state. Slot C: if lowered to Unit, a pure dedup function test can pass while persistence wiring still inserts duplicates (e.g., missing unique check at write path). REJECTION-GATE: considered System, rejected because duplicate suppression here can be validated in-process with local persistence wiring; no real network/subprocess needed.
behaviors.test_description: Use real dedup + queue persistence modules in test configuration. Submit same event ID twice (including near-simultaneous calls if supported) and assert exactly one persisted queue record/enqueue side effect.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "queue reports it is full" and handler "returns 503 with a Retry-After header." Slot B: module handoff boundary (queue capacity signal -> HTTP mapping). Slot C: if lowered to Unit, header/status mapping bugs in handler wiring can be missed even if queue-full detector logic is correct. REJECTION-GATE: considered System, rejected because queue-full signaling and HTTP mapping can be exercised entirely in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Configure queue module/test double to return a concrete "full" error/signal. Call handler via HTTP-layer test harness and assert `503` plus correct `Retry-After` header value.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: Slot A: "persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit." Slot B: real persistence module boundary (capacity enforcement in storage-backed queue, not just in-memory algorithm). Slot C: if lowered to Unit, an in-memory counter test can pass while the real persistent adapter allows overflow due to transaction/order semantics. REJECTION-GATE: considered System, rejected because behavior text targets queue component semantics and can be validated with local DB/filesystem-backed integration, without real network/subprocess.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue backend in test mode with a small capacity, enqueue up to limit, then attempt one more enqueue and assert explicit rejection/error and unchanged persisted depth.
